summary: Phase I reactions are catalysed by cytochrome P450 in the smooth ER and involve oxidation, reduction, or hydrolysis.
Phase I reactions are catalysed by transferases in the rough ER.
Phase I reactions involve conjugation with glucuronic acid.
At toxic doses of paracetamol, the reactive metabolite NAPQI accumulates and depletes hepatic glutathione, causing centrilobular necrosis.
Paracetamol toxicity occurs because glucuronidation produces a toxic metabolite.
NAPQI is a harmless metabolite that is safely excreted in urine.
N-acetylcysteine (NAC) is the antidote for paracetamol toxicity because it replenishes glutathione stores.
Activated charcoal is the definitive antidote for paracetamol toxicity.
NAC works by directly neutralising paracetamol in the stomach.
Superoxide dismutase converts superoxide radicals to hydrogen peroxide, which is then detoxified by catalase or glutathione peroxidase.
Catalase converts superoxide radicals directly to water.
Superoxide dismutase converts hydrogen peroxide to superoxide radicals.
Phase II reactions involve conjugation of xenobiotics with polar molecules (glucuronic acid, sulphate, glutathione) to increase water solubility.
Phase II reactions involve oxidation of xenobiotics by cytochrome P450.
Phase II reactions decrease water solubility of xenobiotics.
Vitamin E (tocopherol) is the major lipid-soluble antioxidant protecting cell membranes from lipid peroxidation.
Vitamin C is the major lipid-soluble antioxidant.
Glutathione is the primary lipid-soluble antioxidant in cell membranes.
Reactive oxygen species (ROS) include superoxide, hydrogen peroxide, and hydroxyl radicals, and are produced as by-products of aerobic metabolism.
ROS are produced only during disease states and are not part of normal metabolism.
ROS are exclusively generated by exogenous toxins.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review xenobiotic metabolism and oxidative stress.
Good effort! Revise the antioxidant defense mechanisms.
Excellent understanding of xenobiotic metabolism and antioxidants!
